coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots embedded metal-organic framework@molecularly imprinted nanoparticles for highly sensitive and selective detection of quercetin
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Longhua
Pan, Mingfei
Fang, Guozhen
Wang, Shuo
coredata.dc:description: A facile and efficient one-pot approach for the synthesis of carbon dots embedded metal-organic framework@molecularly imprinted polymer nanoparticles (CDs@MOF@MIP), which can be used for highly selective and sensitive optosensing of quercetin (QCT), is reported herein for the first time. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with high specific surface area and porosity were first prepared via a room-temperature reaction and used as a matrix to construct MOF-based sensor. Then, highly blue luminescent carbon dots (CDs) were introduced to act as signal transducer that can sense the bonding interactions between the developed sensor and the target molecules and further transduce them to the detectable fluorescence signals. Final, through a surface molecular imprinting process, the resulting CDs@MOF@MIP was obtained. The CDs@MOF@MIP not only exhibited higher selectivity and sensitivity toward target molecular compared to carbon dots embedded metal-organic framework @non-imprinted polymer (CDs@MOF@NIP), but also showed faster reaction rate than CDs-embedded molecularly imprinted polymer (CDs@MIP) derived from the introduction of porous MOF and the optical detection. This proposed sensor was employed to sense trace QCT, and its fluorescence presented a well linear decline with the increasing concentration of QCT from 0 μM to 50.0 μM with a limit of detection of 2.9 nM (S/N = 3), and the precision for eleven replicate detections of 0.5 μM QCT was 1.9%. This developed CDs@MOF@MIP sensor was also used to determine QCT content in real Ginkgo biloba extract capsules with satisfactory performance, and the results were correlated well with those obtained using high performance liquid chromatography.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-organic framework
Carbon dots
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Fluorescent sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Absorption and fluorescence behavior of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP for QCT
Establishment of the FL sensing detection method based on CDs@ZIF-8@MIP for QCT
Sensing QCT in real samples using CDs@ZIF-8@MIP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic presentation of the synthetic procedure for CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and its application in QCT sensing.

UV–vis absorption spectra of QCT(a), CDs@ZIF-8@MIP before(b) and after(c) interaction with QCT, and the stacked curve (d) of (a) and (b), FL excitation (e) and emission (f) spectra of the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP.
Kinetics of the FL response of (a) CDs@ZIF-8 and (b) CDs@ZIF-8@MIP incubated with 45 μM QCT.
FL response of (a) CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and (b) CDs@ZIF-8@NIP toward QCT, isorhamnetin, epicatechin, daidzein and rutin with different initial concentrations (10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 μM).

FL response of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP upon addition of various concentrations of QCT (from top to bottom, 0, 0.05, 0.5, 2.5, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 μM) in ethanol solution. Inset: calibration plots of F0/F of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP vs the concentration of QCT.
Results for the determination of QCT in Ginkgo biloba extract capsules using the developed CDs@ZIF-8@MIP sensor and HPLC method.

row.entry: QCT contents determined by CDs@ZIF-8@MIP fluorescent sensor (μM, mean ± SD, n = 3)
QCT contents determined by HPLC method (μM, mean ± SD, n = 3)

head.ce:title: Carbon dots embedded metal-organic framework@molecularly imprinted nanoparticles for highly sensitive and selective detection of quercetin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Ministry of Education, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety
Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety
Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Food Processing Technology and Quality Control in Shandong Province, College of Food Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University, Tai’an, Shandong 271018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Food Processing Technology and Quality Control in Shandong Province
College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile and efficient one-pot approach for the synthesis of carbon dots embedded metal-organic framework@molecularly imprinted polymer nanoparticles (CDs@MOF@MIP), which can be used for highly selective and sensitive optosensing of quercetin (QCT), is reported herein for the first time. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with high specific surface area and porosity were first prepared via a room-temperature reaction and used as a matrix to construct MOF-based sensor. Then, highly blue luminescent carbon dots (CDs) were introduced to act as signal transducer that can sense the bonding interactions between the developed sensor and the target molecules and further transduce them to the detectable fluorescence signals. Final, through a surface molecular imprinting process, the resulting CDs@MOF@MIP was obtained. The CDs@MOF@MIP not only exhibited higher selectivity and sensitivity toward target molecular compared to carbon dots embedded metal-organic framework @non-imprinted polymer (CDs@MOF@NIP), but also showed faster reaction rate than CDs-embedded molecularly imprinted polymer (CDs@MIP) derived from the introduction of porous MOF and the optical detection. This proposed sensor was employed to sense trace QCT, and its fluorescence presented a well linear decline with the increasing concentration of QCT from 0 μM to 50.0 μM with a limit of detection of 2.9 nM (S/N = 3), and the precision for eleven replicate detections of 0.5 μM QCT was 1.9%. This developed CDs@MOF@MIP sensor was also used to determine QCT content in real Ginkgo biloba extract capsules with satisfactory performance, and the results were correlated well with those obtained using high performance liquid chromatography.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are an extensive class of crystalline materials constructed from metal ions linked together by organic bridging ligands to form one-, two-, or three-dimensional infinite networks [1,2]. Their discovery at the end of the eighties once represented a revolution in chemistry and attracted an increasing number of researchers to participate in this new field due to the immense chemical and structural possibilities this family of materials offers for imaginative chemists. Now, MOFs are becoming one of the most rapidly developing fields in chemical and material sciences [3,4], not only because of their advantageous properties including extremely large surface area, tunable pore sizes, and adjustable internal surface properties [5], but also due to their great potential in many applications such as catalysis [6,7], separation [8], gas storages [9], drug delivery [10,11], and sensing [12]. However, their sensing function has rarely been implemented due to lack of signal transduction capacity. In recent years, much effort has been dedicated to explore the potential of MOFs as chemical sensors by developing various functionalized MOFs [13].

Among them, luminescent MOFs (LMOFs) with the accessible porosity and intrinsic luminescence that can capture and transduce the host-guest chemistry to detectable luminescence changes have recently emerged as an ideal functional material to construct FL sensor [14]. In general, there are two main strategies to synthesize LMOFs. One strategy is using lanthanide ions (such as Ln3+, Eu3+, Tb3+, and Dy3+) as the coordination center to synthesize lanthanide-MOFs (Ln-MOFs) [15–18], which are able to generate visible fluorescence emission arising from f-f or f-d energy transfer tuned by suitable adjacent chromophores when exposed to UV light [19]. Another strategy is encapsulating luminescent guest molecules or nanoparticles into the host frameworks of nonluminescent MOFs, for example, Buso et al. synthesized LMOFs by introducing semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) after appropriate surface functionalization within MOF-5 crystals [20], Lu et al. even put forward a versatile encapsulation strategy that allows several types of nanoparticles to be incorporated within crystals of zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8) materials [21], Saha et al. obtained LMOFs by incubation of CdS quantum dots without any capping agent in a Zn(II) based low-molecular weight metallohydrogel [22], Zhao et al. developed a fluorescent sensing platform for phosphate through combination of MOFs and ZnO quantum dots [23].

Despite the fact that several LMOFs has been synthesized and shown their up-and-coming prospect as luminescent sensors, relatively low luminescence activities of LMOFs limits their further application in real samples due to the difficulty in obtaining LMOFs with high luminescence quantum yield merely through the utilization of the intrinsic luminescence of components or the generated luminescence from the weak metal-ligand charge transfer in MOFs [24], or by introduction of the guest florescent nanoparticles whose FL can be easily quenched during encapsulation or application process. Therefore, the quest for novel luminescent nanomaterial with intrinsic photostability and improved fluorescence QY is ongoing.

Carbon dots, as an emerging luminescent nanoparticles, considering its prominent advantages over semiconductor quantum dots in photoluminescence stability [25], green and easy synthesis [26], low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility [27] as well as good water solubility [28], is expected to be very promising guest fluorescent material to fabricate LMOFs sensors [29]. In previous work by us and others [24,30,31], a few LMOFs sensor by encapsulation of CDs in ZIF-8 has been developed to enhance chemical sensing and thus improve the detection sensitivity. The selectivity of MOF materials for specific analyte, is substantial, but, as yet, is not highly developed, because the design of MOFs directly capable of specifically recognizing the targeted small molecules still remains a formidable challenge owing to the complex nature of the sample matrices.

Molecular imprinting has proven to be a versatile and straightforward method to produce synthetic receptors with specific molecular-recognition sites [32,33]. The resulting molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP) have been reported in MOF-based sensors including electrochemical sensor [34], optical sensor [35] as well as a quartz crystal microbalance sensor [36] to improve their sensing selectivity. Compared to small organic compounds, MIP-based flourscent sensors display their intrinsic signal amplification capability. Moreover, in contrast with molecular sensors, MIP film sensors are easily fabricated into devices [37]. In addition, the flexible chemistry of imprinted polymers allows introduction of several different fluorophore recognition units to produce a valuable conclusion based on the combination of different outputs [38]. Recently, a great amount of fluorescent MIPs based on semiconductor quantum dots [39–42], gold nanoclusters [43], carbon quantum dots [44,45], and graphene quantum dots [46,47] have been developed and shown a broad application future in the field of optical sensing.

Inspired these, a novel fluorescent sensor based on carbon dots embedded metal-organic framework@molecularly imprinted polymer has been developed through an efficient one-pot reaction. Here, ZIF-8, as a prototypical imidazole-based MOFs, which not only possesses high specific surface area and large pores, but also exhibits exceptional chemical and thermal stability [48,49] was used as support, molecularly imprinted polymer on the surface of ZIF-8 provided specific adsorption and recognition sites for target molecular, and the incorporated blue luminescent CDs acted as tentacle to sense the specific binding events, and then transduce it to the detectable fluorescence signals. The as-synthesized CDs@ZIF-8@MIP nanoparticles integrated advantages of MIP, CDs and ZIF-8, and thus exhibited excellent fluorescence intensity and stability, short adsorption equilibrium time, as well as high selectivity and sensitivity toward analyte-QCT, which is a powerful antioxidant and an excellent free-radical scavenger that can be used to prevent and treat some disease associated with high blood pressure and tumors. Moreover, the fluorescence of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP can be effectively quenched by QCT, which is linear over a large dynamic range of 0–50.0 μM with a detection limit of 2.9 nM. This developed FL sensor was successfully employed for the determination of QCT in health food of Ginkgo biloba extract capsules with good accuracy and stability.

Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, 99%), epicatechin (99%) and daidzein (98%) were acquired from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (China). Citric acid anhydrous (AC, 99.5%) and 2-methylimidazole (2-Hmin, 99%) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Regent Co., Ltd (China). N-(β-Aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl methyldimethoxysilane (AEAPMS, 97%), 4-vinylpyridine (4-Vp, 95%), ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA, 97%), 2, 2-azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN, 98%) were bought from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd (Japan). Quercetin (QCT, 99%), rutin (98%), and isorhamnetin (98%) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (China). 4-Vp, EGDMA and AIBN needs purification before using, and the other chemicals were used as received. Samples of Ginkgo biloba extract capsules produced by different manufactures were purchased from health-food stores.

ZIF-8 was synthesized in a typical experiment according to our previous report [30], 1.467 g Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and 3.244 g 2-Hmin were mixed in 400 mL methanol by a magnetic stirrer at ambient conditions, until the mixture turns turbid then kept it for 1 h, the final ZIF-8 nanocrystals were collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, washing with fresh methanol, and drying in a vacuum oven at 50 °C for 10 h.
CDs was prepared via one-step reaction of 10 mL AEAPMS and 0.5 g anhydrous citric acid at 240 °C for 1 min, and the details can be found in early report [50].

CDs@ZIF-8@MIP was synthesized as presented in Scheme 1 , and the detail procedure as follow: fluorescent CDs (250 μL) and support ZIF-8 (50 mg) were dispersed in a mixture of N, N-dimethyl formamide (17.5 mL) and acetonitrile (2.5 mL) by magnetic stirring for 0.5 h, then template QCT (1 mmol) and functional monomer 4-Vp (5 mmol) were added and kept for 1.0 h. Subsequently, cross-linker EGDMA (25 mmoL) and initiator AIBN (50 mg) were added. After stirring for 30 min, the pre-polymerization mixture was degassed by ultrasonication, purged with nitrogen, sealed and then incubated in a water bath at 60 °C for 24 h to complete the polymerization reaction. The obtained CDs@ZIF-8@MIP was washed by Soxhlet extraction in methanol /acetic acid (300 mL, 9:1 v/v) until no template QCT was checked out.
CDs@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP were also synthesized following above procedure but in the absence of support ZIF-8, template QCT, respectively.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was measured with KBr pellets on a Bruker Tensor 37 FT-IR spectrometer (Germany). The surface morphologies of the synthesized materials were observed on a Hitachi SU1510 scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Japan) at an accelerated voltage of 10 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were conducted on a Shimadzu X-6100 X-ray powder diffractometer equipped with a Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 1.5406 Å) in the 2θ range of 5°-60° (Japan). Brunauer Emmett Teller (BET) specific surface area was given out by N2 adsorption experiment on a Quantachrome Autosorb 6 instrument (America). The thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) were performed on a SDTA851Mattler Toledo TGA (Switzerland) in nitrogen atmosphere with a flow rate of 50 mL min−1 and a heating rate of 5 °C min-1 in 25 °C˜1000 °C.

FL measurements were performed on a Hitachi F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan) equipped with a 1 × 1 cm quartz cell using the following conditions: the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, the excitation and emission slit widths of 5 nm, and the photomultiplier tube voltage of 700 V. In order to evaluate the performance of newly fabricated materials, 1.0 mg CDs@ZIF-8@MIP, CDs@ZIF-8@NIP, or CDs@MIP powder was dispersed thoroughly in 3 mL different concentration solution containing QCT or its analogues to measure their FL intensity under above given condition.

Commercial Ginkgo biloba extract capsules were used to investigate the application of this proposed CDs@ZIF-8@MIP sensor in detecting QCT. The samples were firstly removed the capsule, ground into powders, and sieved through a 100-mesh sieve. Finely ground test sample (40.0 mg) and 80% methanol solution (20 mL) were thoroughly mixed and sealed in a conical flask, the mixed system was weighed and sonicated for 20 min. After cooling to room temperature, a complementary amount of 80% methanol solution was added and blended in order to keep the total weight at a constant value. Finally, the blended mixture was filtered through a short-stem funnel, and then the filtrate was collected.

1.0 mL collected filtrate was evaporated to incipient dryness, and dissolved in ethanol to give a volume of 5 mL. 1.0 mg CDs@ZIF-8@MIP was dispersed in 3 mL test solution and incubated for 15 min for FL analysis.

In order to verify the accuracy of analysis results obtained by CDs@ZIF-8@MIP sensor, 20 μL collected filtrate that was filtered through a 0.22-μm nylon microporous membrane was injected into the HPLC system, which consisted of a Waters 600E pump, a UV–vis detector set at 360 nm, 20-μL injection loop, a LC workstation for data collection and C18 reversed-phase column with 250 mm × 4.6 mm i.d. (5 μm, Agilent Technologies). The mobile phase was methanol/0.4% phosphoric acid (50: 50, v/v) with a flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1.

As illustrated in Scheme 1, a novel carbon dots embedded metal-organic framework@molecularly imprinted nanoparticles for selective and sensitive determination of quercetin was prepared through an efficient one-pot polymerization. CDs and ZIF-8 were first added into the solution, owing to its ultra-small size CDs can enter and occupy the internal aperture of the ZIF-8, then the polymerization reaction was conducted with thermal initiation in the water bath, in the presence of template QCT, functional monomer 4-Vp, cross-linker EGDMA and initiator AIBN. After the polymerization process, MIP or NIP film formed on the surface of ZIF-8 mainly depending on physical deposition effect. The synthesized CDs@ZIF-8@MIP was characterized by FT-IR, TEM, XRD, N2 adsorption-desorption measurements, and TGA.

FI-IR analysis was first carried out to verify the formation of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP, the FT-IR spectroscopy of QCT, CDs@ZIF-8@MIP before and after extraction of QCT, and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP were recorded in Fig. S1. The typical peak around 3427 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibration of OH in QCT (Fig. S1a), and such strong absorption may due to the presence of intramolecular hydrogen bonds between group −OH and CO in QCT molecular. With adding of functional monomer of 4-Vp, the intermolecular hydrogen bonds between QCT and 4-Vp came into existence and occupied a dominant position, so the absorption peak of −OH happened blue shift in QCT-template CDs@ZIF-8@MIP (Fig. S1b). After removing of QCT (Fig.S1c), the IR absorption peaks of −OH revert to longer wave numbers, more importantly, the characteristic peaks of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP are almost identical to that of CDs@ZIF-8@NIP (Fig.S1d) . For example, the peaks at 3438 cm−1 and 3608 cm−1, 2989 cm-1 and 2958 cm−1, and 1728 cm−1 could be ascribed to the stretching vibration of −OH, CH, CO, respectively. And the peaks at 1635 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 are the characteristic absorption of CN and CC in heterocyclic benzenoid. The peaks at 1456 cm-1 and 1390 cm-1 are resulted from the bending vibration of C–H, while 1257 cm-1 and 1159 cm-1 could be assigned to the absorption of COC from carboxylate ester. The peaks at 952 cm-1, 879 cm-1, and 817 cm-1 was attributed to the bending vibration of alkene, and the peaks at 464 cm-1 was attributed to the presence of Zn-N group. These typical signals of a CN, CC and ZnN indicate ZIF-8 have been embedded in the resulting material, and the typical signals of a COC further prove that the polymerization reaction between 4-Vp and EGDMA have been successfully completed. And those similar pecks in CDs@ZIF-8@MIP (Fig. S1c) and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP (Fig. S1d) indicates their similar chemical composition and the effective removing of template molecule.

The surface morphology of ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP was observed by SEM and TEM, and the images were presented in Fig. S2 and Fig. S3. The ZIF-8 crystals (Fig. S2a) have a relatively smooth surface with uniform size, CDs@ZIF-8@MIP (Figs. S2c, S3b) and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP (Figs. S2b, S3c) shows a typical core-shell structure, and they still keep the primary framework originated from ZIF-8 (Fig. S3a) while their surface are coated with a thin film about 30 nm resulted from polymerization process.

The crystal structure of synthesized ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP were also investigated by powder XRD analysis in Fig. S4. The reflection located at 2θ = 7.3° is attributed to a 110 peak (Fig. S4a), which agrees well with the fact that the nanocrystals of ZIF-8 with rhombic dodecahedron (Fig. S3a) are composed of 12 facets [51]. However, it worth noting that after the polymerization procedure, this diffraction peak of ZIF-8 almost disappeared and a new broad characteristic diffraction d 200 peak located at 2θ = 13.5° appeared in CDs@ZIF-8@MIP (Fig. S4b) and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP (Fig. S4c) which originates from the successful coating of MIP and NIP.

The TGA analysis was conducted in a stream of nitrogen to assess the thermal stability of the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP. As observed in Fig. S5, the thermal gravimetric curve in CDs@ZIF-8@MIP was almost the same as that in CDs@ZIF-8@NIP indicating the complete removing of template molecule in MIP layer, and they also showed a very highly thermal stability without any weight loss in 25–250 °C. The decomposition of the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP started from 250 °C and plateaued around 450 °C with about 96.2% of weight loss due to the decomposition of framework and organic polymer chain, but for CDs@ZIF-8@NIP this process mainly happened in 250–430 °C and then continued to 700 °C with a total weight loss of 94.06%. Compared to the pure ZIF-8 crystal [52], the trend of weight loss in both CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP obviously became tardive, which demonstrates the MIP layer on ZIF-8 plays an important role in improving the thermal stability of the single phase MOFs.

N2 adsorption–desorption measurements were also carried out to determine the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller specific surface area of CDs@MIP, CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP. As expected, CDs@ZIF-8@MIP featured a significantly higher BET area of 879.43 m2 g−1 than CDs@MIP (123.05 m2 g−1) and CDs@ZIF-8@NIP (776.07 m2 g−1) due to the introduction of porous ZIF-8 and imprinted cavities left after removal of the template, which may provide more accessible sites for template recognition and binding.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption and fluorescence behavior of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP for QCT
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption behavior of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP toward QCT was investigated. In order to verify the effectiveness of the introduction of MOFs in CDs@ZIF-8@MIP, a series of absorption experiments was designed by using CDs@MIP as a contrast.

First, the binding properties of the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@MIP toward QCT were studied, as shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescence of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@MIP were found to be quenched, and the quenched degree (F0/F) both showed an obvious increasing along with the QCT content increasing, when the QCT concentration was 50 μM, the F0/F of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP reached 5.33, which is 1.8 times of CDs@MIP, which demonstrates the important effect of ZIF-8 in improving the adsorption capacity of the composite material. And the FL quenching mechanism was also discussed by investigating the interaction between CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and QCT, as depicted in Fig.1 , the UV absorption spectrum of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP after interaction with QCT (Fig.1c) was almost coincide with the stacked spectrum (d) of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP (b) and QCT (a), meanwhile there is no obvious new peaks that marking the complexes formation appearing, and the UV absorption spectra of QCT (a) has no overlap with fluorescence emission spectra (f) of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP but has an significant overlap with its FL excitation spectra (e), based on those behavior, the fluorescence quenching can be speculated as an dynamic process resulted from the inner filter effect between CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and QCT.

Next, the adsorptive dynamic experiment was conducted by adding 1.0 mg CDs@ZIF-8@MIP or CDs@MIP material into 3 mL ethanol solution or QCT ethanol solution (45 μM) to measure their FL intensity after different incubation times at room temperature. As can be seen in Fig. 2 , the FL response (F0/F) was used to evaluate adsorption capacity, so the F0/F vs absorption time was drawn to reflect the adsorption rate of synthesized CDs@ZIF-8@MIP and CDs@MIP to QCT. In Fig. 2a, compared to CDs@MIP (Fig. 2b), the adsorption capacity of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP toward QCT was dramatically increased meanwhile the adsorption equilibrium time was largely shortening from 210 min to 15 min, the good performance can be attributed to the introduction of ZIF-8 in the polymer, ZIF-8 with high specific surface area provides an ideal platform to form MIP membrane to allow more imprinted sites released from the deep buried polymer networks compared to the CDs@MIP prepared by bulk polymerization, and thus significantly improve the binding capacity and mass transfer rate. The CDs@ZIF-8@MIP was expected to be employed for rapidly bonding and enrichment of QCT.

Third, the adsorptive selectivity of CDs@ZIF-8@MIP to QCT was also studied in Fig. 3 , here isorhamnetin, epicatechin, daidzein and rutin (Fig. S7) were chosen as structural and functional analogs of QCT. CDs@ZIF-8@MIP was found to bind more template molecules (QCT) than their corresponding CDs@ZIF-8@NIP (see in Fig. 3a and b) and showed higher selectivity towards QCT (see in Fig.4 a) than other analogs, which suggests the presence of specific binding sites in the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP. It is interesting that different from traditional non-imprinted materials, CDs@ZIF-8@NIP still showed a certain degree of selectivity to QCT than other anlages, which can be ascribed to the FL selectivity that come from carbon dots itself. This synergistic selectivity from MIPs and CDs inevitably make CDs@ZIF-8@MIP materials possess high recognition accuracy toward QCT. As a result, CDs@ZIF-8@MIP was identified as an ideal fluorescent material that can be used for highly sensitive and selective QCT sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of the FL sensing detection method based on CDs@ZIF-8@MIP for QCT
ce:section.ce:para: A dose dependent interaction between FL response (F0/F) and QCT concentration was confirmed through a fluorescence quenching titrations experiment by adding CDs@ZIF-8@MIP at different concentrations of ethanol solutions of QCT. As shown in Fig. 4, the FL intensity sharply decreased along with the increase of QCT concentration, a good linear relationship F0/F = 0.0887 CQCT + 1.0322 was found for CDs@ZIF-8@MIP over a QCT concentration range of 0˜50.0 μM with correlation coefficients of 0.9981 from the calibration plots depicted in insets of Fig. 4, which follows a modified Stern-Volmer equation, where F0 and F are the FL intensities of the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP suspension solution in the absence and presence of QCT, respectively, and CQCT represents the concentration of QCT, and the quenching constant (Ksv) of 0.0887 was also obtained. The detection limit of QCT was calculated to be 2.9 nM (S/N = 3) and the precision (RSD) for eleven replicate detections of QCT (0.5 μM) was 1.9%, which showed that the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP sensor have very promising application in highly sensitive determination of QCT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing QCT in real samples using CDs@ZIF-8@MIP
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application of the constructed CDs@ZIF-8@MIP sensor was also tested through determination of QCT in Ginkgo biloba extract capsules from different manufacturers. The contents of QCT in five samples were detected to be 3.43, 4.16, 2.14, 2.29 and 5.67 μM using our developed CDs@ZIF-8@MIP prober (Table 1 ), which was in good agreement with the results obtained by the conventional HPLC-UV method, demonstrating this developed sensor has good accuracy and reproducibility and can be used for sensing QCT in real samples.

In this work, an efficient one-pot strategy has been put forward to construct a novel carbon dots-embedded, MOF supported molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensor. This fabricated CDs@ZIF-8@MIP sensor integrated the advantages of MOFs, CDs and MIP, thereby not only having strong and fast fluorescence response to QCT originated from CDs and MOFs, but also endowing this response with high sensitivity and selectivity benefiting from MIP shell, which can be employed for sensing QCT over a wide concentration (0˜50.0 μM) with a low LOD (2.9 nM). More importantly, the current study provided a new general and efficient method to construct multifunctional sensing platform for a wide range of analytes, and could be used further to advance progress in the rapid detection technology on the spot.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Tianjin Municipal Science and Technology Commission, China (Project No. 17ZYPTJC00050), National Key Research and Development Program of China (Project No. 2017YFC1600803) and International Science and Technology Cooperation Program of China (Project No. 2014DFR30350).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.01.156.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The new age of MOFs and of their porous-related solids
The chemistry and applications of metal-organic frameworks
Metal-organic frameworks: versatile heterogeneous catalysts for efficient catalytic organic transformations
Framework-topology-dependent catalytic activity of zirconium-based (porphinato)zinc(II) MOFs
Metal-organic framework nanosheets in polymer composite materials for gas separation
Reticular synthesis of HKUST-like tbo-MOFs with enhanced CH4 storage
3D porous crystalline polyimide covalent organic frameworks for drug delivery
One-pot synthesis of metal-organic frameworks with encapsulated target molecules and their applications for controlled drug delivery
A series of multifunctional metal-organic frameworks showing excellent luminescent sensing, sensitization, and adsorbent abilities
Metal-organic frameworks as platforms for functional materials
MOF based luminescence tuning and chemical/ physical sensing
ratiometric nanothermometer based on an emissive Ln3+-organic framework
